claims: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous system capable of generating novel research hypotheses, implementing experiments in code, and producing complete scientific manuscripts without human intervention, closing the loop from idea to paper.

SciAgentBench (2024) introduced a comprehensive benchmark of 338 research-level scientific tasks across 44 diverse disciplines to evaluate LLM agents on authentic scientific problem-solving, finding that top agents solve fewer than 10% of tasks.

MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized evaluation framework for LLM agents performing machine learning research tasks including literature review, experiment design, and iterative refinement, revealing systematic failure modes in long-horizon planning.

HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024) showed that iterative hypothesis refinement using LLM agents guided by experimental feedback outperforms single-shot generation, achieving statistically significant improvements on drug discovery and materials science benchmarks.

AlphaFold 3 (Abramson et al., 2024) extended structure prediction to biomolecular complexes including DNA, RNA, and small molecules using a diffusion-based architecture, establishing a new paradigm for AI-driven structural biology that accelerates drug target identification.

OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) demonstrated that retrieval-augmented LLM agents grounded in a 45-million-paper corpus outperform GPT-4 on scientific question answering while dramatically reducing hallucination rates, setting a new standard for literature-grounded AI assistants.

ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) introduced an iterative multi-agent pipeline in which specialized sub-agents handle literature retrieval, idea generation, and critique, showing that structured agent collaboration yields more novel and feasible research ideas than single-agent baselines.

AI peer review systems evaluated in Liang et al. (2024) revealed that GPT-4-based reviewers agree with human reviewers at rates comparable to inter-human agreement on NeurIPS and ICLR submissions, while also identifying systematic biases toward longer, more confident-sounding papers.

Autonomous Chemical Research with Coscientist (Boiko et al., 2024 follow-up work) scaled to multi-step organic synthesis planning and robotic execution, successfully completing reactions that required integrating web search, database queries, and hardware control without human intervention.

SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) established a rigorous benchmark of 338 scientific coding problems derived from real research papers in physics, biology, and chemistry, finding that frontier models including GPT-4o solve fewer than 5% of multi-step problems requiring domain knowledge integration.

DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) formalized data-driven scientific discovery as a benchmark task requiring agents to form and test hypotheses from structured datasets, revealing that current LLMs fail to reliably distinguish spurious from causal relationships.

LLM-based automated literature synthesis (Wang et al., 2024) demonstrated that multi-agent systems can produce systematic reviews of comparable quality to human-written reviews on narrow biomedical topics, as judged by domain experts blinded to authorship.

Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging approach (2024) showed that evolutionary algorithms can autonomously combine foundation model weights to produce specialized scientific models without gradient-based training, achieving competitive performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

BioPlanner (O'Donoghue et al., 2024) introduced a benchmark and agent system for autonomous biological experimental design, showing that LLM agents can generate valid experimental protocols rated as feasible by wet-lab biologists in over 60% of cases.

Nobel Turing Challenge framing (Krenn et al., 2025) articulated a concrete long-range research agenda for AI systems capable of making Nobel-Prize-level discoveries autonomously, synthesizing lessons from 2024 agent systems to identify key capability gaps in causal reasoning, embodied experimentation, and scientific communication.

AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous research pipeline — from idea generation through experiment execution, paper writing, and automated peer review — producing novel machine learning findings without human intervention.

SciAgentBench (2024) established a benchmark of 338 real scientific tasks across 44 domains, showing that frontier LLMs solve fewer than 5% autonomously, quantifying the gap between current agents and genuine scientific competence.

ChemCrow (Bran et al., 2023/2024) showed that integrating 13 chemistry tools into an LLM agent (GPT-4) enabled autonomous planning and execution of multi-step organic synthesis, including successful synthesis of a novel chromophore.

OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) introduced a retrieval-augmented system over 45M open-access papers that outperformed GPT-4 on scientific literature QA by grounding every claim in retrieved passages, reducing hallucination rates by ~50%.

MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized environment for evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end ML engineering tasks, finding Claude-2 and GPT-4 succeed on only ~30% of tasks, with failures concentrated in debugging and iterative experimentation.

Autonomous Chemical Research Agent (Boiko et al., 2023) demonstrated that GPT-4 connected to web search and lab robotics could autonomously design and execute chemical experiments, successfully synthesizing three compounds without human guidance.

LLM-based hypothesis generation benchmark (Wang et al., 2024) showed that GPT-4 and Claude-3 generate scientifically plausible but rarely novel hypotheses compared to human experts, with novelty scores averaging 30% lower on blind expert evaluation.

SciCode (Huang et al., 2024) released a benchmark of 338 coding problems extracted from research-grade scientific software spanning physics, biology, and chemistry, finding that the best models (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5) solve fewer than 5% of the hardest subproblems.

ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) demonstrated an iterative system that queries academic knowledge graphs (Semantic Scholar) to autonomously refine research ideas, achieving higher expert novelty and feasibility scores than single-shot LLM ideation.

Automated peer review systems (e.g., ReviewerGPT, SEA, 2024) were shown to match junior-reviewer acceptance-decision accuracy (~65%) but fail on detecting methodological flaws, confirming LLMs can triage but not replace expert review.
